text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer

---

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) harboring germline and somatic BRCA2 biallelic loss, presenting for follow-up after recent PSMA PET/CT imaging demonstrating radiographic progression on olaparib, including new visceral metastases. He is here today to review imaging results, discuss his current clinical status, and determine next steps in treatment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed approximately three years ago with de novo Stage IVB prostate adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with bone metastases (L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala) and pelvic lymph node involvement, and a presenting PSA of 187.4 ng/mL. He was treated with ADT (leuprolide acetate) combined with abiraterone acetate/prednisone (Zytiga/prednisone) as first-line therapy for metastatic hormone-sensitive disease, with an excellent initial PSA response to nadir of 0.8 ng/mL. He subsequently developed castration resistance approximately two years into therapy, with PSA rising to 8.6 ng/mL and evidence of worsening bone pain. Germline testing at that time identified a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT), and somatic tumor profiling (bone biopsy NGS) confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss along with TP53 p.R175H, AR amplification, and RB1 loss.

He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily as second-line AR pathway inhibitor therapy. He had an initial PSA response to 1.9 ng/mL but progressed radiographically at 7 months with new bone lesions at the right femoral neck, T11, and left acetabulum, as well as new PSMA-avid pelvic lymph nodes on PSMA PET/CT. Enzalutamide was discontinued and he was started on olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg twice daily, given his BRCA2 biallelic loss and PROfound trial eligibility, along with denosumab for skeletal protection.

Olaparib was initiated approximately 11 months ago. He had a meaningful early response with PSA declining from 18.2 to a nadir near 3.9 ng/mL, and the 6-month restaging PSMA PET/CT demonstrated partial response in the bone metastases. However, he developed significant treatment-related myelosuppression over the course of therapy, requiring dose reductions from 300 mg BID to 200 mg BID (at 4 months), then to 150 mg BID (at 8 months) following a Grade 3 anemia episode requiring packed red blood cell transfusion. At 9 months, he experienced a second Grade 3 anemia (Hgb 7.1 g/dL) and Grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets 68 K/uL) despite darbepoetin alfa support, requiring a second transfusion and another hold of olaparib. Hematology was consulted; myelosuppression was attributed to olaparib-associated bone marrow toxicity.

Now at 11 months after starting olaparib, restaging PSMA PET/CT and dedicated CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed in the interim confirm frank radiographic progression with new visceral metastases. Key imaging findings include:
- New PSMA-avid hepatic lesion, right lobe segment VII, 1.4 cm, SUVmax 8.9 (new since prior imaging)
- New PSMA-avid left lower lobe pulmonary nodule, 0.9 cm, SUVmax 6.2
- Three new bone lesions (right femur shaft, left posterior 6th rib, C7 vertebral body) beyond those identified at prior progression imaging
- Re-enlargement of right external iliac lymph nodes (now 1.6 cm, previously reduced to 0.4 cm on olaparib)
- PSA at time of imaging: 31.7 ng/mL, rising significantly from nadir

Today the patient reports new right upper quadrant discomfort that he rates as 2/10 in severity, which he notes began approximately 3 weeks ago. He also endorses persistent fatigue, which he grades as moderate, limiting his activities beyond light household tasks. He has had approximately 8 pounds of unintentional weight loss over the past three months. He denies frank jaundice, nausea, or vomiting. Bone pain at known metastatic sites is present but 3/10 overall. He has bilateral lower extremity paresthesias (prior taxane-like neuropathy from prior denosumab/disease-related changes, though he has not received prior taxane chemotherapy in this clinical course -- his neuropathy is mild and likely related to disease burden). He is ambulating with a cane at home.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Fatigue (moderate/Grade 2), right upper quadrant discomfort (mild, 2/10), diffuse bone pain (3/10, primarily lumbar), bilateral lower extremity paresthesias (mild, Grade 1), unintentional weight loss (~8 lbs over 3 months), reduced appetite.
Negative: Denies fever, chills, rigors, dyspnea at rest or significant exertional dyspnea (mild with moderate exertion), chest pain, hemoptysis, hematuria, gross hematochezia, melena, jaundice, scleral icterus, confusion, syncope, headache, seizures, new neurological deficits, lower extremity edema, rash.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Well-appearing but visibly fatigued, older gentleman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Mild temporal wasting noted.
Vital Signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 88 bpm (regular), RR 16/min, O2 saturation 97% on room air, Temperature 37.0 C, Weight 74.1 kg (down from 77.8 kg three months ago).
ECOG Performance Status: 2 (ambulatory, capable of self-care, but unable to carry out any work activities; up and about more than 50% of waking hours).
HEENT: Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx clear. No oral mucositis. Mild conjunctival pallor consistent with anemia.
Neck: No supraclavicular, cervical, or axillary lymphadenopathy. No jugular venous distension.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or focal crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant and right hypochondrium. No rebound or guarding. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below the right costal margin, firm. No appreciable splenomegaly. Bowel sounds present and active throughout.
Musculoskeletal: Lumbar spine tenderness to percussion, consistent with known metastatic disease. Mild tenderness over right iliac crest. No lower extremity edema. No obvious joint effusions.
Neurological: Alert, oriented, conversant, appropriate affect. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Mild symmetric decreased vibration sense bilateral lower extremities to mid-shin. Strength 4/5 proximal lower extremities bilaterally. Gait mildly antalgic with cane; no focal neurological deficits.
Skin: No jaundice, rash, or ecchymoses. No petechiae. Alopecia consistent with known treatment effects.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (today):
PSA: 31.7 ng/mL (markedly elevated from nadir of 3.9 ng/mL on olaparib)
Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (castrate)
CBC:
  - WBC 3.8 K/uL (low-normal; ANC estimated 2.1 K/uL)
  - Hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL (Grade 2 anemia; last transfusion approximately 3 weeks ago, post-transfusion Hgb had been 9.4 g/dL)
  - MCV 96 fL
  - Platelets 92 K/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, recovering from nadir of 68)
  - Reticulocyte count 0.5%
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR ~51 mL/min/1.73m2 - stable chronic kidney disease)
  - ALT 48 U/L (mildly elevated, upper limit of normal ~40), AST 52 U/L (mildly elevated), Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL (normal), Alkaline phosphatase 184 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone metastases), Albumin 3.3 g/dL (mildly decreased)
LDH: 298 U/L (mildly elevated)
Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1, aPTT 31 sec (normal)
Darbepoetin alfa given 3 weeks ago; next dose due.

---

IMAGING REVIEW:
Recent PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) and dedicated CT chest/abdomen/pelvis confirm unequivocal radiographic disease progression on olaparib therapy. As reviewed in detail above:
- New liver lesion in segment VII (1.4 cm), PSMA-avid with SUVmax 8.9 -- this is visceral metastatic disease, a new finding representing a qualitative change in disease pattern
- New left lower lobe pulmonary nodule (0.9 cm), PSMA-avid -- also representing new visceral involvement
- Multiple new bone lesions (right femur shaft, left posterior 6th rib, C7) beyond prior known sites
- Progressive pelvic lymphadenopathy
- All lesions strongly PSMA-avid, confirming PSMA-expressing disease burden
There is no salivary gland or lacrimal gland involvement. No leptomeningeal disease. No epidural extension identified on the current imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT:

1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) -- BRCA2 germline (c.5946delT) and somatic biallelic loss, TP53 p.R175H, AR amplification, RB1 loss -- now with confirmed visceral disease progression on olaparib (Lynparza) after approximately 11 months of therapy. PSA 31.7 ng/mL. Disease has spread to liver and lung (new visceral metastases), in addition to progressive bone and lymph node burden. This represents a qualitatively more aggressive disease course with visceral involvement, which carries a less favorable prognosis. Prior therapies: ADT + abiraterone (first-line mHSPC, approximately 24 months), enzalutamide (second-line mCRPC, 7 months), olaparib (third-line, 11 months with initial response and subsequent progression).

2. Treatment-related myelosuppression -- persistent Grade 2 anemia (Hgb 8.8 g/dL) and Grade 1 thrombocytopenia (platelets 92 K/uL) in the setting of olaparib-associated bone marrow toxicity, compounded by bone marrow replacement from extensive osseous metastatic disease. Multiple prior dose reductions of olaparib; olaparib currently held in the setting of progression and myelosuppression workup. Two prior packed red cell transfusions. On darbepoetin alfa with suboptimal response. LFTs mildly elevated, likely combination of hepatic metastasis and residual marrow/nutritional effects.

3. Performance status decline -- ECOG PS 2, worsening over recent months from baseline ECOG 1, likely attributable to cumulative disease burden, bone pain, anemia-related fatigue, and unintentional weight loss. Functional limitations are meaningful in guiding treatment selection.

4. Nutritional compromise -- 8-lb unintentional weight loss over 3 months, albumin 3.3 g/dL, reduced appetite. Consistent with progressive disease and treatment-related factors. Will require nutritional support and close monitoring.

PLAN:

1. Discontinue olaparib. Given clear visceral disease progression on PSMA PET/CT, continued administration of olaparib at any dose is not clinically appropriate. Olaparib will be formally discontinued as of today. The patient and family were counseled regarding the rationale for stopping therapy and the transition to a new treatment approach.

2. Next-line treatment discussion. An extensive goals-of-care conversation was held today with Mr. Kowalski and his family (daughter present). We discussed his disease course, current status, and realistic expectations for further treatment. The following options were reviewed in detail:

   a. Platinum-based chemotherapy: Given his BRCA2-deficient tumor biology, there is biological rationale and retrospective/emerging prospective data supporting platinum sensitivity in HRR-deficient mCRPC. A regimen of carboplatin (dosed to AUC 4-5) combined with docetaxel (Taxotere) is the most commonly used platinum-based doublet in this setting. This approach would target both the BRCA2-related HR deficiency and provide systemic cytotoxic coverage of visceral disease. Potential toxicities include myelosuppression (particularly relevant given his prior anemia history), peripheral neuropathy (he has mild pre-existing paresthesias), alopecia, nausea, fatigue, and risk of febrile neutropenia. G-CSF support would be employed prophylactically. Given his visceral crisis risk from new hepatic metastases, this is the most urgently indicated active cytotoxic regimen.

   b. Lu-177-PSMA-617 (lutetium vipivotide tetraxetan; Pluvicto): Mr. Kowalski's disease is strongly PSMA-avid on recent imaging (all lesions PSMA-positive, no significant PSMA-negative sites), making him technically eligible for Lu-177 PSMA therapy per VISION trial criteria (post-AR pathway therapy and post-taxane, though he has not received prior docetaxel yet in this case - to be clarified regarding eligibility per current label). Lu-177 PSMA-617 has demonstrated survival benefit in PSMA-positive mCRPC. However, given the presence of new visceral metastases (liver and lung) and more rapidly evolving disease, the panel response rate of Lu-177 may not be sufficient to control visceral progression in the immediate term. Additionally, his persistent myelosuppression may be exacerbated by radioligand therapy. This option may be more appropriate after platinum-based chemotherapy if disease remains PSMA-avid and performance status permits.

   c. Cabazitaxel (Jevtana): While approved for post-docetaxel mCRPC, Mr. Kowalski has not received prior docetaxel. Some data support cabazitaxel as a taxane option. However, given the availability of the carboplatin/docetaxel combination leveraging his platinum sensitivity, and his prior myelosuppression history, cabazitaxel is not preferred at this juncture.

   d. Clinical trial: Mr. Kowalski should be evaluated for clinical trial eligibility, particularly trials targeting BRCA2-mutant mCRPC with visceral involvement, combined PARP inhibitor + platinum combinations, or novel AR-pathway combinations. Tumor board discussion will be sought.

   e. Best supportive care / palliative focus: This was discussed openly. Given ECOG PS 2 with recent trajectory toward PS 3, and the burden of treatment over 3 years, the option of transitioning to purely symptom-directed palliative care without further systemic therapy is valid and respected. Mr. Kowalski expressed a strong desire to pursue further active treatment if his body can tolerate it, and he understood the risks and benefits. He and his family feel that the potential for tumor response and extended time justifies the toxicity risk at this point.

   Decision: After thorough discussion, we will proceed with carboplatin + docetaxel chemotherapy as the most appropriate next-line treatment given: (a) visceral crisis risk from hepatic metastases, (b) BRCA2-driven platinum sensitivity rationale, (c) patient's desire for continued active treatment, and (d) PSMA-directed therapy preserved as a future option. We will target carboplatin AUC 4 and docetaxel 60 mg/m2 every 21 days, with prophylactic G-CSF (filgrastim) given his prior myelosuppression history and ECOG PS 2.

3. Supportive care and referrals:
   - Palliative care consultation: Placed urgently given evolving prognosis, pain management needs, and family support requirements. Palliative care team will be actively co-managing symptom burden.
   - Nutrition/dietitian referral: For unintentional weight loss and declining albumin; discuss nutritional supplementation strategies.
   - Bone protective therapy: Denosumab 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks to be continued (last dose administered 3 weeks ago; next dose on schedule). Calcium and vitamin D supplementation continued.
   - Leuprolide acetate (LHRH agonist): Continued at 22.5 mg IM every 3 months to maintain castrate testosterone; next injection due in 6 weeks.
   - Pain management: Scheduled non-opioid analgesics (acetaminophen) with breakthrough opioid (oxycodone 5 mg PO PRN) prescribed for bone pain management. Pain team involvement if escalation needed.
   - Darbepoetin alfa: Will continue at current dosing schedule pending chemotherapy initiation (to be reassessed; ESA use may be adjusted once chemotherapy-related anemia context is re-established).
   - Social work: Engaged for transportation, caregiver support, and community resources.
   - CBC and CMP: Recheck in 1-2 weeks prior to chemotherapy initiation (cycle 1). Platelet recovery to >100 K/uL and Hgb stability required before starting carboplatin.

4. Advance care planning: The patient has a healthcare proxy designated (daughter, present today). We reviewed his current advance directive. He wishes for full resuscitation measures at this time but has documented a preference for comfort-focused care if he progresses to a state where he is no longer able to meaningfully interact with family. He understands this may be revisited. He was offered social work support for family meeting facilitation.

5. Patient education: Patient and family were provided written materials regarding carboplatin + docetaxel chemotherapy, expected side effects (myelosuppression, neuropathy, hair loss, fatigue, nausea), safety precautions (infection, bleeding), and when to call or present to emergency care. Instructions given to call immediately for fever >38.0 C, unusual bleeding, severe pain, confusion, or inability to swallow.

6. Follow-up: Return in approximately 3 weeks for cycle 1 of carboplatin + docetaxel, pending laboratory clearance. Interim call from oncology nurse coordinator in 1 week to assess symptom status and lab review.

---
Time spent with patient and family in this visit: 60 minutes (more than 50% counseling and coordination of care).

Electronically signed by: [Attending Oncologist, MD]
Department of Medical Oncology
